summary: Jeff Atwood catalogs common blog and internet clichés that have become overused, drawing from a Gawker post and reader comments. He acknowledges being guilty of some himself while arguing that catchphrases can be fun in moderation but become problematic when used unconsciously. The post advocates for self-awareness in writing and intentionally varying one's expression rather than relying on predefined patterns. Atwood compares catchphrases to exclamation points—rarely necessary but powerful when timed for maximum impact. He suggests using tools like an online cliché finder to audit your writing and encourages writers to stretch beyond comfortable linguistic habits.
key_points: Clichés and catchphrases become problematic when writers don't realize how often they're using them
Using a catchphrase is like ending a sentence with an exclamation point: rarely necessary, but time it for maximum impact
Good communication requires intentionally mixing things up and finding fresh ways to express ideas
Self-awareness is the key differentiator between effective catchphrase use and lazy writing
There are countless ways to communicate any idea, so limiting yourself to predefined patterns is a choice
money_quotes: Using a catchphrase is like ending a sentence with an exclamation point: rarely necessary, but when it is, time it for maximum impact.
Most people don't realize how often they're using a catchphrase, which is why they become overused and cliché in the first place.
I refrain from using the same words, the same phrases, the same stuff too often.
There are a million ways to communicate any idea. Why limit yourself to narrow, predefined patterns of thinking, writing, and speaking?
Stretch a little. Explain it a different way this time. Rephrase. Restate. Riff on the topic.
Using clichés is fine; just be sure you aren't using them as a substitute for real communication.
key_insight: Clichés aren't inherently bad, but unconscious reliance on them signals lazy thinking—good writers stay aware of their linguistic habits and intentionally vary their expression.
